text:
2022 BCCA 6 R. v. St. Constantine
COURT OF APPEAL FOR BRITISH COLUMBIA
Citation:
R. v. St. Constantine,
2022 BCCA 6
Date: 20220111
Docket: CA47170
Between:
Regina
Respondent
And
Gabriale Rene St. Constantine
Appellant
Before:
The Honourable Madam Justice Fisher
The Honourable Mr. Justice Abrioux
The Honourable Mr. Justice Marchand
On appeal from: An order of the Provincial Court of British Columbia, dated October 16, 2020 (sentence) (R. v. St. Constantine, Surrey Docket 240463‑2‑C).
Counsel for the Appellant:
A.A. Goldin
Counsel for the Respondent:
R. Leong
Place and Date of Hearing:
Vancouver, British Columbia
December 3, 2021
Place and Date of Judgment:
Vancouver, British Columbia
January 11, 2022
Written Reasons by:
The Honourable Mr. Justice Marchand
Concurred in by:
The Honourable Madam Justice Fisher
The Honourable Mr. Justice Abrioux
Summary:
The appellant pleaded guilty to one count of returning to Canada without authorization after having been deported, contrary to the Immigration and Refugee Protection Act. He challenges his sentence of nine months' incarceration on the basis that the sentence is demonstrably unfit. The appellant argues that the sentencing judge overemphasized deterrence, failed to give proper effect to proportionality and parity, and failed to consider collateral consequences associated with COVID‑19. Held: Appeal dismissed. The sentencing judge appropriately emphasized deterrence, which is paramount for IRPA offences, and did not err in her application of the principles of proportionality, parity or collateral consequences. The appellant's nine‑month sentence is towards the top of the range but is not demonstrably unfit.
Reasons for Judgment of the Honourable Mr. Justice Marchand:
I. Introduction
[1] On October 16, 2020, Gabriale Rene St. Constantine entered a guilty plea to one count of returning to Canada without authorization after having been deported, contrary to s. 52(1) of the Immigration and Refugee Protection Act, S.C. 2001, c. 27 [IRPA], thereby committing an offence under s. 124(1)(a) of the IRPA. He was sentenced the same day to nine months' incarceration less time served of 47 days.
[2] Mr. St. Constantine appeals his sentence. He submits the sentencing judge erred by overemphasizing deterrence, failing to give proper effect to the principles of proportionality and parity, and failing to consider collateral consequences associated with COVID‑19. Mr. St. Constantine submits the judge imposed a demonstrably unfit sentence.
II. Circumstances
[3] Mr. St. Constantine is an American citizen. On September 14, 2020, Mr. St. Constantine unlawfully entered Canada. At that time, he was prohibited from returning to Canada without authorization by virtue of two previous deportation orders. He was arrested by Canada Border Services Agency ("CBSA") officers and taken to the Pacific Highway Processing Centre. While there he sought, but later abandoned, a claim for refugee status.
[4] Nine hours after his arrest, a third deportation order was issued to Mr. St. Constantine. He was escorted to the border and returned to the United States. Twenty minutes after his deportation and removal, Mr. St. Constantine again entered Canada unlawfully. He was promptly arrested. He candidly admitted that, despite his arrest, he intended to enter Canada again. He indicated that he preferred being in jail in Canada to being in the United States.
[5] Mr. St. Constantine remained in custody until his sentencing hearing. His sentence has now been served and he has been returned to the United States.
III. Reasons for Sentence
[6] After briefly setting out the circumstances, the sentencing judge summarized a number of important objectives and principles of sentencing found in ss. 718-718.2 of the Criminal Code [Code]. The Crown proceeded by indictment, so these provisions of the Code applied by operation of s. 34(2) of the Interpretation Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. I‑21.
[7] The judge then reviewed the submissions of the parties, summarizing various authorities cited by them, including R. v. Luckstein, 65 B.C.A.C. 154, 1995 CanLII 2350 (C.A.); R. v. Williams (2 March 2006), Vancouver 170581‑2C (B.C.P.C.); R. v. Polnac, 2017 BCSC 2408; R. v. Dosson, 2018 BCSC 1688; and R. v. Suarez, 2019 QCCA 649.
[8] The judge next addressed "individualized considerations." She noted that Mr. St. Constantine was 59 years old, had a diploma in computer science, and had resided in various locations in the United States, including most recently at a homeless shelter in Eugene, Oregon. She also noted that Mr. St. Constantine did not suffer from either mental health or substance abuse issues and had a dated criminal record in the United States.
[9] The judge identified two mitigating factors, namely Mr. St. Constantine's guilty plea and his expression of remorse.
[10] The judge did not accept Mr. St. Constantine's explanation that he entered Canada as a result of fear due to political unrest and violence in the State of Oregon. Without minimizing the unrest, in the absence of evidence that Mr. St. Constantine "as a Caucasian citizen of the United Sates of Jewish faith" had personally experienced intimidation, threats, harassment or violence due to the unrest, she found "no nexus" between the unrest and Mr. St. Constantine.
[11] The judge also did not accept Mr. St. Constantine's explanation that, due to his age and medical conditions, he felt he would be much safer from COVID‑19 in Canada than in the United States. Based on the extensive reading Mr. St. Constantine asserted he had done, the judge concluded he "would have been well aware that Covid is a global pandemic," that the border was closed and that the United Sates "presented Mr. St. Constantine with a vast array of options to ensure his personal well‑being and safety" within his own country.
[12] The judge identified a number of aggravating factors. After noting that Mr. St. Constantine had been deported from Canada in the past, she found that he had flagrantly disregarded the laws of Canada by re‑entering the country only 20 minutes after having been deported and put himself and others at risk by disregarding COVID‑19-related health restrictions and protocols. The judge characterized Mr. St. Constantine's actions as "selfish."
[13] The judge acknowledged she had to be "mindful of the risk of Covid" within correctional facilities. At the same time, she had to balance that risk against Mr. St. Constantine's selfish and flagrant misconduct. She adopted a passage from R. v. Hearns, 2020 ONSC 2365 at para. 23, that any direct or collateral consequences of the pandemic "cannot justify a sentence that is disproportionately lenient, or drastically outside of the sentencing range." Ultimately, the judge was not convinced that a period of incarceration would expose Mr. St. Constantine to significant collateral consequences or make the sentence she would be imposing disproportionate.
[14] The judge took guidance from a pre‑pandemic, unreported decision of hers that dealt with somewhat similar circumstances. In that decision, she emphasized that Canada and the United States enjoy the longest undefended border in the world. She held that "[t]he integrity and safety of our borders is paramount" and "uphold[ing] the law, including immigration policies and procedures, is important in imposing a fit and just sentence addressing deterrence and denunciation." (I note that the judge's unreported decision was overturned on appeal, but not on these points: R. v. Singh, 2017 BCSC 1277.)
[15] Finally, the judge concluded:
[31] In carefully considering the facts before me, the statutes applicable, the case law, the personalized mitigating and aggravating factors before me, and the uniqueness of the global pandemic as a collateral consequence [of] which I must be mindful, I will impose a period of incarceration upon Mr. St. Constantine of 9 months. This shall be less 32 days in custody, and with enhanced credit [it will] be 47 days. While this is a step up from his last conviction, it is clear that Mr. St. Constantine is not appreciating the severity of his actions, both in committing the offence once again, but also the irony that he is seeking an application of Covid when considering his period of incarceration, even though he chose to put himself, the CBSA officers and citizens of this country at risk. To also re‑enter this country some mere 20 minutes after spending nine hours in custody, is a flagrant disregard and disrespect for the laws of this country. In crafting the sentence, I am mindful of the principles of sentencing that the uniqueness of this offence places emphasis on both general and specific deterrence.
IV. Standard of Review
[16] Sentencing is a highly individualized process. Sentencing judges have front‑line experience, see and hear all the evidence and submissions in person, and are generally familiar with the circumstances and needs of the local community. As a result, sentencing judges have broad discretion to impose a fit sentence. Sentencing decisions are entitled to considerable deference and an appeal court should only substitute its own decision for a sentencing judge's for good reason: R. v. Friesen, 2020 SCC 9 at para. 25; R. v. Sellars, 2018 BCCA 195 at para. 22.
[17] When weighing competing sentencing objectives, it falls to the sentencing judge to determine which deserve the greatest weight: R. v. Voong, 2015 BCCA 285 at para. 12, citing R. v. Nasogaluak, 2010 SCC 6 at para. 43. An appeal court may not vary a sentence simply because it would have imposed a different one: Voong at para. 11, citing R. v. L.M., 2008 SCC 31 at para. 14.
[18] An appeal court can intervene to vary a sentence only if the sentence is demonstrably unfit or if the sentencing judge made an error in principle that had an impact on the sentence: Friesen at para. 26; Sellars at para. 22; R. v. Lacasse, 2015 SCC 64 at para. 41.
V. Analysis
A. Did the sentencing judge err in principle by overemphasizing deterrence?
[19] Mr. St. Constantine submits that the sentencing judge's emphasis on deterrence was "not balanced" and "unreasonable." In particular, Mr. St. Constantine submits the judge misquoted Suarez. At issue is the following passage from the judge's reasons for sentence:
[18] In the Suarez appeal, the Court noted the individualized process in crafting an appropriate sentence, including balancing the sentencing objectives and addressing proportionality. The Court, however, noted the importance of these offences and that the emphasis must be on deterrence to " send a clear message to any foreigners who might be tempted to act ." (para. 13), and further noted at para. 23,
As for the public interest, which is [an] infinitely variable concept, it must take into account not only the general deterrence objective, but also the gravity of the offence and its impact on the community.
[20] In Suarez, the Court of Appeal for Quebec set aside a 45‑day jail sentence imposed on the appellants and substituted a conditional discharge. The appellants had entered Canada unlawfully. They had previously made an unsuccessful refugee claim, were removed from Canada and were not entitled to return for one year. Within that one‑year period, they re‑entered Canada and sought permanent resident status via sponsorship and on humanitarian and compassionate grounds. The appellants did not have criminal records, they did not make any false statements, this was their first unlawful entry, and pre‑sentence and psychological reports had recommended a discharge.
[21] In its reasons for judgment, the Court did not take issue with the sentencing judge's finding that the usual range of sentence for a first offence of unlawful entry is a custodial sentence of 30 days to six months. Nevertheless, the Court held that the sentencing judge erred in principle by relying on deterrence to exclude any possibility of a conditional discharge. The Court explained:
[12] For the reasons set out below, we find that the judge committed an error in principle and that the sentence imposed in the case at bar is demonstrably unfit, because it disregards the particular situation of the appellants.
[13] Indeed, in order to send a clear message to any foreigners who might be tempted to act as the appellants did, the judge excluded any possibility of ordering a conditional discharge for this type of offence, without considering the best interests of the appellants and the actual impact of his decision:
[TRANSLATION]
[29] Granting a discharge would amount to encouraging foreigners seeking entry to Canada to do so by flouting Canadian laws or the decisions rendered by Canadian authorities against them, which is what the two accused did.
[21] [A] sentence of 45 days of imprisonment will result in the appellants having to wait 10 years, outside Canada, before being able to file a sponsorship application, a consequence that will not occur if a conditional discharge is ordered.
[22] Clearly, it is in the best interests of the appellants that a conditional discharge be ordered.
[23] As for the public interest, which is an infinitely variable concept, it must take into account not only the general deterrence objective, but also the gravity of the offence and its impact on the community. Professors Parent and Desrosiers conclude that: [TRANSLATION] "Stated succinctly, the likelihood of obtaining a discharge is inversely proportional to the gravity of the offence viewed through the prism of the circumstances of the case".
[24] Deterrence should not automatically preclude a discharge, particularly where the consequences for the accused would be severe, as in the present case, and where the offence is a less serious one.
[25] Based on the facts in the case at bar, the judge should have seriously considered the consequences of a sentence of imprisonment. Had he done so, he would have ordered a conditional discharge.
[22] These excerpts support Mr. St. Constantine's submission that the judge misquoted Suarez. She portrayed the Court of Appeal's summary of the sentencing judge's reasoning at para. 13 as the Court's own reasoning. She also took the quote from para. 23 out of context and mischaracterized its intent. Paragraphs 22-25 deal with the requirements that a discharge only be granted if the court "considers it to be in the best interests of the accused and not contrary to the public interest": Code, s. 730(1). Read in context, para. 23 was intended to reinforce the message that deterrence should not preclude the consideration of a discharge. It was not intended to emphasize the gravity of the offence of unlawful entry.
[23] While the judge misquoted Suarez, I am unable to say her misunderstanding had a material effect on the sentence she imposed. It was common ground at the sentencing hearing, as it is on appeal, that deterrence is the paramount sentencing objective in cases such as Mr. St. Constantine's that involve a deliberate breach of the IRPA. This conclusion is well‑supported by the authorities, including from this Court: see e.g., Luckstein and Polnac. Likewise, the Court of Appeal for Quebec in Suarez did not dispute the importance of deterrence. The Court simply held the sentencing judge erred in principle by relying on it to exclude any consideration of an available sentencing option, namely a conditional discharge.
[24] I would not accede to this ground of appeal.
B. Did the sentencing judge err in principle by failing to give proper effect to proportionality?
[25] Proportionality is a fundamental principle of sentencing that ensures a sentence reflects the gravity of the offence and the moral blameworthiness of the offender: Code, s. 718.1. Consideration of the gravity of the offence promotes justice for victims and ensures public confidence in the justice system. Consideration of the moral blameworthiness of the offender serves a restraining function that ensures justice for the offender: R. v. Ipeelee, 2012 SCC 13 at para. 37.
[26] As noted, sentencing is a highly individualized process. In determining a fit sentence, the sentencing judge must take into account relevant aggravating and mitigating circumstances (Code, s. 718.2(a)), as well as objective and subjective factors related to the offender's personal circumstances: R. v. Pham, 2013 SCC 15 at para. 8.
[27] Mr. St. Constantine submits the sentencing judge erred in assessing proportionality by rejecting his subjective reasons for entering Canada, supported as they were by the well‑known state of the COVID‑19 pandemic and race‑related civil unrest in the United States. Mr. St. Constantine notes that his subjective reasons were not contested by the Crown. Mr. St. Constantine also submits the sentencing judge over‑emphasized the flagrant manner of his unlawful entries into Canada without restraint and without seeing them as a single desperate transaction "devoid of any sophistication."
[28] I agree that it is possible for a person such as Mr. St. Constantine to believe COVID‑19-related conditions in Canada are better than in the United Sates and to fear civil unrest, even if not personally victimized. However, I am unable to agree the judge failed to give proper effect to proportionality.
[29] A fair reading of the reasons for sentence demonstrate that the judge considered Mr. St. Constantine's subjective reasons for entering Canada. Even though the Crown did not contest Mr. St. Constantine's explanations, the judge was not obliged to accept his explanations or give them any weight: R. v. Sangha, 2014 BCCA 379 at para. 22; R. v. Read, 2016 BCCA 111 at para. 27. She measured Mr. St. Constantine's purported beliefs against well‑known facts about the COVID‑19 pandemic and civil unrest in the United States. In the result, she was "not convinced" that Mr. St. Constantine had demonstrated a reasonable belief. While the judge could have expressed herself better regarding the lack of any possible nexus between civil unrest in the United States and Mr. St. Constantine's asserted fear, she nevertheless reached a conclusion that was open to her.
[30] The judge also considered Mr. St. Constantine's submission that his re‑entry into Canada was akin to a "tantrum." Certainly, Mr. St. Constantine's conduct was unsophisticated and perhaps even childish. His conduct ensured he would be arrested. Indeed, that appears to have been his goal. The judge was nevertheless entitled to take a dim view of Mr. St. Constantine's conduct. She was unconvinced of the sincerity of his subjective beliefs and took into account the importance of heightened border security aimed at controlling the spread of COVID‑19.
[31] In my view, Mr. St. Constantine has not demonstrated any error in principle in the judge's approach to proportionality. The real question is whether she erred in her application of the applicable principles. The answer to that question depends, in large part, on whether the sentence she imposed lines up with sentences imposed on similarly situated offenders. I will deal with that issue next.
C. Did the sentencing judge err in principle by failing to give proper effect to parity?
[32] In Friesen, the Supreme Court of Canada explained the complementary relationship between parity and proportionality:
[32] Parity and proportionality do not exist in tension; rather, parity is an expression of proportionality. A consistent application of proportionality will lead to parity. Conversely, an approach that assigns the same sentence to unlike cases can achieve neither parity nor proportionality (R. v. L.M., 2008 SCC 31, [2008] 2 S.C.R. 163, at paras. 36-37; R. v. Ipeelee, 2012 SCC 13, [2012] 1 S.C.R. 433, at paras. 78-79).
[33] In practice, parity gives meaning to proportionality. A proportionate sentence for a given offender and offence cannot be deduced from first principles; instead, judges calibrate the demands of proportionality by reference to the sentences imposed in other cases. Sentencing precedents reflect the range of factual situations in the world and the plurality of judicial perspectives. Precedents embody the collective experience and wisdom of the judiciary. They are the practical expression of both parity and proportionality.
[33] Mr. St. Constantine submits that the sentencing authorities relied on by the sentencing judge demonstrate that the sentence she imposed violates the parity principle. I disagree.
[34] In Luckstein, the offender had been convicted of second‑degree murder. He was subsequently granted parole on the condition that he be deported to Germany. Soon after his deportation, he returned to Canada, apparently to help his wife sell their mobile home. This Court rejected the submission that a sentence of fifty per cent of the maximum should be reserved for cases involving serious aggravating factors such as multiple acts of re‑entry or further offending while unlawfully in Canada. Emphasizing deterrence, this Court upheld the 12‑month custodial sentence imposed by the sentencing judge.
[35] In Williams, the offender had both Canadian and American criminal records. He was sentenced to a 12‑month custodial sentence following his fourth unlawful entry conviction. On his previous three convictions, he received 25‑day, 30‑day and nine‑month custodial sentences. The circumstances of the three previous convictions are not entirely clear from the decision, but the fourth conviction was aggravated by the fact that he had been found in possession of cocaine. As noted by the sentencing judge in Williams:
[21] [W]hat is clear from [the] authorities is that multiple convictions under the Immigration Act can result in some very significant sentences . It's also clear from [the] authorities that in terms of the Immigration Act, and this particular offence, the key consideration is not rehabilitation, because this person is going to be leaving our country, and it's not reparations to the community, it's deterrence, specific deterrence to the accused, and deterrence to other people. The message to them must be that they are not to come into this country, having been deported, unless they have permission from the Minister to return.
[36] In Polnac, the offender had six prior deportations and four prior convictions for unlawfully entering Canada. He had a wife and children in Canada. His prior convictions resulted in custodial sentences of one day plus 68 days time served, four months, six months plus four months time served, and one day plus 13 months time served. On his fifth conviction, he received the maximum sentence of two years, with most of that having already been served. Given the offender's history of offending and complete lack of remorse, the court emphasized specific deterrence. The court also emphasized general deterrence, noting there is no wall between Canada and the United States, so American citizens must understand there will be penalties if they surreptitiously enter Canada.
[37] In Dosson, the offender was convicted by a jury of unlawfully entering Canada, obstructing an immigration officer and assaulting a peace officer. After being deported, the offender was subsequently refused entry into Canada and told to return to the United States. He refused to do so voluntarily and had to be restrained and handcuffed. Several months later, he presented himself at the border without appropriate authorization and spat on an immigration officer in an interview room. In the absence of mitigating factors, the court imposed the custodial sentences sought by the Crown. The offender was sentenced to three months for the immigration offence, two months concurrent for the obstruction offence and three months consecutive for the assault offence, all of which had already been served. The court commented that the sentences sought by the Crown were at the low end of the range.
[38] Returning to Mr. St. Constantine's case, I cannot say that the judge erred by imposing a sentence that offended the parity principle. The judge took into account Mr. St. Constantine's guilty plea and expression of remorse. She was entitled to disregard what she considered to be Mr. St. Constantine's unreasonable explanations for unlawfully entering Canada. While there is no suggestion that Mr. St. Constantine was attempting to enter Canada to engage in criminal activity, Mr. St. Constantine had been subject to three previous deportation orders and had one prior conviction. What truly sets Mr. St. Constantine's case apart is the fact that he returned to the border a mere 20 minutes after having been deported and expressed that he intended to continue to do so, all in the middle of a pandemic that required heightened restrictions on our unprotected border.
[39] No case was cited during the hearing of Mr. St. Constantine's appeal that establishes the range of sentence for the offence of unlawfully entering Canada in this jurisdiction. That is likely because the circumstances of unlawful entry cases can vary so widely. Nevertheless, the cases that were cited to the sentencing judge and to this Court support multi‑month custodial sentences for offenders unlawfully entering Canada in particularly aggravated circumstances, even on a first offence.
D. Did the sentencing judge err in principle by failing to consider collateral consequences associated with COVID-19?
[40] At the sentencing hearing, Mr. St. Constantine raised the issue of COVID‑19-related collateral consequences. He referred the sentencing judge to the frequently cited decision of Hearns. In Hearns, Justice Pomerance undertook a thorough analysis of the impact of the COVID‑19 pandemic on sentencing principles. She noted two obvious impacts of the pandemic on those receiving custodial sentences, namely increased health risks and adverse effects on conditions of imprisonment due to, for example, increased lockdowns to reduce the risk of infection. Justice Pomerance specifically held that:
[15] [c]learly, the pandemic does not do away with the well‑established statutory and common law principles. However, the pandemic may impact on the application of those principles. It may soften the requirement of parity with precedent.
[23] The consequences of a penalty -- be they direct or collateral -- cannot justify a sentence that is disproportionately lenient, or drastically outside of the sentencing range. It cannot turn an inappropriate sentence into an appropriate one or justify dispositions that would place the public at risk. It is ultimately a question of balance.
[41] Mr. St. Constantine says the judge erred by: (1) restricting her view of the collateral consequences to the question of whether Mr. St. Constantine was at increased medical risk of contracting the coronavirus; and (2) improperly treating Mr. St. Constantine's submissions on the pandemic as an aggravating factor.
[42] In Pham, the Supreme Court of Canada addressed how the collateral consequences of a sentence may be taken into account. The Court held, at para. 11, that a collateral consequence relates to the impact of a sentence on a particular offender.
[43] Collateral consequences are not aggravating or mitigating factors as they do not relate to the gravity of an offence or the moral blameworthiness of an offender. Rather, they are to be taken into account as personal circumstances of an offender. "Their relevance flows from the application of the principles of individualization and parity": Pham at para. 11. Their weight varies from case to case and "should be determined having regard to the type and seriousness of the offence": Pham at para. 12. While a sentencing judge has discretion to take collateral consequences into account, the sentence must ultimately remain proportionate to the gravity of the offence and the degree of responsibility of the offender: Pham at para. 14.
[44] In this case, it is true that the sentencing judge did not explicitly address Mr. St. Constantine's submissions on the impact of heightened restrictions in correctional facilities during the pandemic. It is also true that she considered it contradictory for Mr. St. Constantine to seek a reduction in his sentence on account of the pandemic when the fact of the pandemic was an aggravating factor.
[45] Mr. St. Constantine's case came before the busiest court in British Columbia, the Surrey Provincial Court, during the heart of the pandemic. Despite the challenges presented by the heavy caseload in Surrey and the pandemic, the sentencing judge rendered considered reasons for judgment. She did not refer to every argument advanced by the parties, nor was she obliged to: R. v. Dinardo, 2008 SCC 24 at para. 30; R. v. Burns, [1994] 1 S.C.R. 656 at 664-665, 1994 CanLII 127. Mr. St. Constantine was very likely not the first offender to cite Hearns (which had been decided six months earlier) or make submissions about the collateral consequences of the pandemic to the sentencing judge.
[46] In all of the circumstances, I am not prepared to conclude that the judge ignored Mr. St. Constantine's submissions on the impact on incarcerated offenders of more restrictive conditions in correctional facilities. On my reading of the judge's reasons for sentence as a whole, it is apparent that she did not give much, if any, weight to the impact. Weighing the impact was well within her purview as the front‑line judge responsible for imposing a fit sentence.
E. Did the sentencing judge impose a demonstrably unfit sentence?
[47] Mr. St. Constantine submits that the sentencing judge imposed a demonstrably unfit sentence that was a "marked departure" from sentences imposed for the same offences on similarly situated offenders. He also submits that the sentence offends the "step‑up" principle.
[48] I have already addressed Mr. St. Constantine's argument that his sentence was a marked departure from previous sentencing authorities. In short, it was not. It was open to the judge to take a dim view of Mr. St. Constantine's conduct. She was entitled to reject his explanations for unlawfully entering Canada and to characterize his behaviour as both "flagrant" and "selfish." Mr. St. Constantine had been the subject of a number of previous deportations and had one prior conviction for unlawfully entering Canada. He demonstrated an intention to ignore Canadian laws and the directions of CBSA officers. He did all of this in the middle of a pandemic, when the statutory goal of protecting public health and safety and maintaining the security of Canadian society took on heightened importance: IRPA, s. 3(1)(h).
[49] The step‑up principle is grounded in the sentencing objective of rehabilitation and the circumstances of the particular offender. It is based on the theory that sentence increases should be moderate so as to not discourage the offender from rehabilitating themself due to the offender viewing the sentence as "a dead weight on his future life": R. v. Robitaille (1993), 31 B.C.A.C. 7 at para. 9, 1993 CanLII 2561.
[50] I acknowledge that Mr. St. Constantine's sentence was a significant step‑up from his previous sentence, but the primary sentencing objective in the circumstances of this case was deterrence, not rehabilitation. I am therefore unable to conclude that Mr. St. Constantine's sentence offended the step‑up principle.
VI. Disposition
[51] The judge imposed a sentence that was toward the top end of the range. In doing so, she took a defensible view of the circumstances. She appropriately emphasized deterrence and did not err in her application of the principles of proportionality, parity and collateral consequences. Mr. St. Constantine has failed to establish that the sentence he received was demonstrably unfit.
[52] I would dismiss the appeal.
"The Honourable Mr. Justice Marchand"
I AGREE:
"The Honourable Madam Justice Fisher"
I AGREE:
"The Honourable Mr. Justice Abrioux"
